title: Decision tree pruning with latency constraints
description: Determine whether a decision tree can be pruned so that all root-to-leaf paths satisfy a given latency or depth constraint

examples.input: Tree: root with left child (leaf) and right child (leaf). Latency constraint: 1
examples.explanation: 1. Start at root. Option 1: Keep root → check paths: root-left (depth 2) violates constraint (2 > 1). Option 2: Remove root → entire tree pruned, no paths exist, vacuously satisfies constraint. Return True.

examples.input: Tree: root with left child (has leaf grandchild) and right child (leaf). Latency constraint: 2
examples.explanation: 1. Root: try keep. Left child: try keep → leaf grandchild path depth 3 > 2, invalid. Left child: try remove → prunes left subtree, right child path depth 2 ≤ 2, valid. But must check root keeping: right child path depth 2 okay. However, need to verify all combinations: actually exploring fully: Root keep → left keep → grandchild leaf depth 3 invalid; left remove → right leaf depth 2 valid → return True for left subtree. So overall root keep with left remove works. Wait, re-evaluate: Actually the problem asks if ANY pruning works. Let's trace properly: Root: option keep. Then left child: option keep → check leaf grandchild depth 3 > 2 → invalid. Left child: option remove → prunes left subtree, then check right leaf depth 2 ≤ 2 → valid. So root keep with left remove works → return True. My initial output was wrong. Correct output: True. Trace corrected: Root keep, left remove, right keep gives paths: only right leaf depth 2 meets constraint.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursive Check
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all possible pruning combinations by checking each node for removal or retention, and validate if all resulting root-to-leaf paths meet the latency constraint. This is a naive method that exhaustively considers every subtree configuration.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Decision Tree Pruning with Latency Constraints
ladders.desc: Complete solution checking if a decision tree can be pruned so all root-to-leaf paths satisfy a latency constraint.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem where we need to determine if a decision tree can be pruned to meet latency constraints. The brute force approach explores every possible pruning combination by recursively deciding at each node whether to keep it or remove it (which prunes its entire subtree). After each pruning decision, we must validate that all remaining root-to-leaf paths have depth ≤ latency constraint. The intuition is exhaustive search: we systematically try all 2^n possible configurations (where n is number of nodes) to find if any valid pruning exists. The algorithm starts at the root, recursively explores both keeping and removing each node, and checks constraints at leaf nodes or when subtrees are removed. Time complexity is O(2^n) because each node has 2 choices (keep/remove), and space is O(h) for recursion stack where h is tree height. Connection to L1: This uses the recursive exploration function from L1 as its core engine. Common mistakes include not properly handling the case where removing a node prunes its entire subtree, or forgetting to check constraints at intermediate points. Real code pattern: define a recursive function that returns whether a valid pruning exists for current subtree, trying both options at each node.

examples.input: Tree: root with left child (leaf) and right child (leaf). Latency constraint: 1
examples.trace: 1. Start at root. Option 1: Keep root → check paths: root-left (depth 2) violates constraint (2 > 1). Option 2: Remove root → entire tree pruned, no paths exist, vacuously satisfies constraint. Return True.

But must check root keeping: right child path depth 2 okay. However, need to verify all combinations: actually exploring fully: Root keep → left keep → grandchild leaf depth 3 invalid; left remove → right leaf depth 2 valid → return True for left subtree. So overall root keep with left remove works. Wait, re-evaluate: Actually the problem asks if ANY pruning works. Let's trace properly: Root: option keep. Then left child: option keep → check leaf grandchild depth 3 > 2 → invalid. Left child: option remove → prunes left subtree, then check right leaf depth 2 ≤ 2 → valid. So root keep with left remove works → return True. My initial output was wrong. Correct output: True. Trace corrected: Root keep, left remove, right keep gives paths: only right leaf depth 2 meets constraint.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Exploration of Pruning Combinations
ladders.desc: Recursive function that explores keep/remove decisions at each node to generate all pruning configurations.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that implements the brute force search. At each node, we have two choices: keep the node (and recursively process its children) or remove the node (which prunes its entire subtree). The function returns whether there exists at least one valid pruning configuration for the subtree rooted at current node that satisfies the latency constraint. The intuition is depth-first search with branching: we explore both possibilities at each node, combining results from children appropriately. When we keep a node, we must ensure all child subtrees can be pruned validly; when we remove, we immediately satisfy constraints for that subtree (since no paths remain). Step-by-step: Base cases: if node is None, return True (empty tree trivially valid). Recursive case: try remove option → return True immediately (pruning entire subtree). Try keep option → if current depth > latency constraint, return False; otherwise recursively check all children; if all children return True for keep option, return True; else return False. Time: O(2^n) worst-case as it explores all combinations. Space: O(h) recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called from the main solution to explore the entire tree. Common mistakes: not handling the remove option correctly (should return True without checking children), or not checking depth constraint before recursing on keep option. Real code pattern: def can_prune(node, depth, latency): if not node: return True; if depth > latency: return False; # Try remove option: return True; # Try keep option: for child in node.children: if not can_prune(child, depth+1, latency): return False; return True.

examples.input: Node: root with two leaf children, depth=0, latency=1
examples.trace: 1. Node root at depth 0. Try remove: return True immediately. But we need to explore keep option too for completeness. Actually function should try both: remove returns True, keep: check depth 0 ≤ 1 okay. Recurse left child depth 1: try remove → True; keep: depth 1 ≤ 1 okay, child is leaf so no children → return True. Similarly right child. So keep option returns True. Overall function returns True (since remove or keep works). Wait, my output is wrong. Let's trace correctly: Function returns True if EITHER remove OR keep works. Here remove works → return True. But the example input says output False? I need consistent example. Let's use latency=0: root depth 0 ≤ 0 okay. Left child depth 1 > 0 so keep option fails. Remove option works → return True. Still True. To get False, need case where both remove and keep fail. Example: root with leaf child, latency=0, but if we remove root, entire tree gone → vacuously True. So maybe my example is bad. Let's redefine: Node: root with leaf child, depth=0, latency=0. Trace: Try remove → True. So output True. Hmm. Actually for this sub-routine, it always has remove option which returns True, so it always returns True? That's not right for the overall problem. I see the issue: In full problem, remove option at root means pruning entire tree, which might be valid, but for sub-routine we need to integrate with parent checks. Let me correct: This sub-routine should not have remove option returning True always; instead, it should explore both and return True if any works. But remove option is valid only if it doesn't break parent constraints. I think I'm confusing levels. For L1, it's the recursive exploration function that returns boolean. Let's use a clear example: Tree: root with leaf child. Latency=0. Function at root: depth 0 ≤ 0 okay. Try keep: recurse to child depth 1 > 0 → returns False. Try remove: returns True. So overall True. But for full problem, this might be acceptable. I'll adjust example to have output True. New example: Input: root with leaf child, depth=0, latency=0. Output: True. Trace: as above.

examples.trace: 1. Root depth 0 ≤ 1. Try keep: recurse left child depth 1: try keep → leaf, depth 1 ≤ 1, no children → return True; similarly right child → return True. So keep option returns True. Try remove also returns True. Overall True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Branching on Keep/Remove Decisions with Constraint Validation
ladders.desc: The fundamental technique of trying both keeping and removing a node, and validating path constraints.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core decision-making logic: at each node, we branch into two possibilities—keeping the node or removing it—and validate constraints accordingly. When we keep a node, we must ensure the current depth does not exceed the latency constraint, and then recursively apply the same logic to children. When we remove a node, we effectively prune its entire subtree, meaning no paths pass through it, so constraints are vacuously satisfied for that subtree. The intuition is combinatorial search: by making independent decisions at each node, we generate all possible pruned tree configurations. The validation ensures that only configurations where all root-to-leaf paths have depth ≤ latency are considered valid. Step-by-step: For a node at depth d: 1. If d > latency, keeping is invalid. 2. Option Keep: if d ≤ latency, recursively check all children; valid only if all children can be pruned validly. 3. Option Remove: always valid for the subtree (since no paths remain). The algorithm combines these with OR logic: valid if either keep or remove works. Time complexity for this logic at one node is O(1) plus recursive calls, but overall leads to exponential time. Space is O(h). Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the recursive function. Common mistakes: forgetting to check depth before recursing on keep option, or incorrectly handling the remove option as invalid. Real code pattern: if depth > latency: keep_valid = False; else: keep_valid = all(can_prune(child, depth+1, latency) for child in children); remove_valid = True; return keep_valid or remove_valid.

examples.input: Node at depth 2, latency=3, has two children
examples.trace: 1. Depth 2 ≤ 3, so keep option possible. Check children recursively. Remove option always True. So return True.

examples.input: Node at depth 3, latency=2, has no children
examples.trace: 1. Depth 3 > 2, so keep option invalid. Remove option True. Return True.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal with Depth Tracking
ladders.desc: Traversing a tree while maintaining current depth from root.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a tree (e.g., decision tree) and keeping track of the depth of each node relative to the root. Depth is defined as the number of edges from root to the node. In tree traversal algorithms like DFS or BFS, we increment depth as we move to children. The intuition is that to check latency constraints, we need to know how deep each node is, as the constraint limits the maximum depth of any root-to-leaf path. Step-by-step: Start at root with depth 0. Recursively visit each child, passing depth+1. For leaf nodes, depth represents the length of the path from root. This building block is essential because the pruning algorithm needs to compare node depths against the latency constraint. Time complexity for traversal is O(n) to visit all nodes, space O(h) for recursion. Connection to L2: Depth tracking provides the 'd' value used in the keep/remove decision logic. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in depth calculation (e.g., counting nodes instead of edges), or not resetting depth properly when backtracking. Real code pattern: def traverse(node, depth): if not node: return; process(node, depth); for child in node.children: traverse(child, depth+1).

examples.input: Tree: root with left leaf and right leaf
examples.trace: 1. Start at root depth 0. 2. Traverse left child: depth 0+1=1. 3. Traverse right child: depth 0+1=1.

examples.input: Tree: root with left child (has leaf grandchild)
examples.trace: 1. Root depth 0. 2. Left child depth 1. 3. Leaf grandchild depth 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking if a Path Satisfies Latency Constraint
ladders.desc: Comparing the depth of a root-to-leaf path against a maximum allowed latency.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves taking a path from root to leaf in a tree and verifying that its depth (number of edges) does not exceed a given latency constraint. The intuition is simple: latency constraint sets a maximum allowed depth for any path; we need to check if a specific path meets this limit. Step-by-step: For a path represented as a sequence of nodes from root to leaf, count the number of edges (which is number of nodes minus 1). Compare this count to the latency constraint; if depth ≤ latency, the path satisfies the constraint; otherwise, it violates it. In practice, we often check this at leaf nodes during traversal by comparing current depth to latency. Time complexity is O(1) per path check. Space O(1). Connection to L3: This operation uses the depth tracked during traversal to perform the check. Common mistakes: confusing depth with number of nodes, or using strict inequality incorrectly. Real code pattern: def path_satisfies(depth, latency): return depth <= latency.

examples.input: Path depth: 3, latency: 5
examples.trace: 1. Depth 3 ≤ 5 → True.

examples.input: Path depth: 4, latency: 3
examples.trace: 1. Depth 4 ≤ 3? No → False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Tree Depth and Root-to-Leaf Paths
ladders.desc: Basic concepts of tree depth and what constitutes a root-to-leaf path.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for beginners. A tree is a hierarchical data structure with nodes connected by edges. The root is the topmost node. A leaf is a node with no children. Depth of a node is the number of edges from the root to that node (root has depth 0). A root-to-leaf path is a sequence of nodes starting at the root and ending at a leaf, following parent-child connections. The length of such a path is its depth (i.e., the depth of the leaf node). Intuition: In decision trees, each path represents a sequence of decisions leading to an outcome; latency constraint limits how long these decision sequences can be. Step-by-step: To find depth of a node, start at root, count edges as you move down. To identify a root-to-leaf path, start at root, recursively go to children until reaching a leaf. Time to compute depth is O(d) where d is depth; space O(1). Connection to L4: This provides the definitions needed to check path constraints. Common mistakes: thinking depth counts nodes instead of edges, or confusing paths with subtrees. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self): self.children = []; def depth(node): if node is root: return 0; else: return 1 + depth(parent).

examples.input: Tree: root with two leaf children
examples.output: Depths: root=0, leaves=1; Paths: root->left, root->right
examples.trace: 1. Root has depth 0. 2. Left child: one edge from root, depth 1. 3. Right child similarly depth 1. 4. Paths: from root to left leaf (2 nodes, 1 edge), root to right leaf.

examples.input: Tree: single node
examples.output: Depth: 0; Path: root (since it's also a leaf)
examples.trace: 1. Single node is root and leaf. Depth 0. Path is just the node itself.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Bottom-Up DP with Memoization
approaches.summary: Perform a post-order traversal to compute, for each node, the minimum and maximum path lengths to leaves in its subtree, and use memoization to decide pruning based on whether these lengths can be adjusted to satisfy the constraint. This avoids redundant calculations by storing results for subtrees.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Decision Tree Pruning with Latency Constraints
ladders.desc: Determine if a decision tree can be pruned so all root-to-leaf paths satisfy a given latency constraint using bottom-up DP with memoization.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires checking whether a binary decision tree can be pruned (by removing subtrees) so that every remaining root-to-leaf path has length ≤ L (latency constraint). The bottom-up DP approach works by performing a post-order traversal to compute, for each node, the minimum and maximum possible path lengths from that node to leaves in its subtree after optimal pruning. The intuition is that we can decide locally at each node whether to keep or prune subtrees based on whether we can adjust path lengths to satisfy L. For each node, we compute (min_len, max_len) where min_len is the shortest possible path to a leaf if we prune aggressively, and max_len is the longest possible path if we keep as much as possible while still satisfying L. The recurrence: For a leaf node, both min_len and max_len are 0 (path length from node to itself). For an internal node with left child (l_min, l_max) and right child (r_min, r_max), we consider two options: 1) Prune one child entirely (set its contribution to infinity, meaning we cannot reach a leaf through that side), or 2) Keep both children. We compute new_min = 1 + min(l_min, r_min) and new_max = 1 + max(l_max, r_max), but only if both children can be adjusted to satisfy L (i.e., l_max ≤ L and r_max ≤ L). If a child cannot satisfy L even after pruning (its min_len > L), we must prune that entire subtree. We store results in a memoization dictionary to avoid recomputation. The tree can be pruned successfully if the root's max_len ≤ L. Time complexity is O(n) since we visit each node once, and space is O(n) for recursion stack and memoization. Connection to L1: This uses the helper function compute_lengths() recursively. Common mistakes include not handling cases where one child must be pruned entirely, or incorrectly updating min/max. Real code pattern: Define a recursive function that returns (min_len, max_len) or a sentinel for impossible nodes.

examples.input: Tree: root with left leaf and right leaf, L=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Post-order traversal starts at left leaf. Leaf node: min_len=0, max_len=0. Step 2: Right leaf: min_len=0, max_len=0. Step 3: Root node: left child (0,0), right child (0,0). Both children satisfy L (0 ≤ 1). Compute new_min = 1 + min(0,0) = 1, new_max = 1 + max(0,0) = 1. Root result: (1,1). Since max_len=1 ≤ L=1, return True.

examples.input: Tree: root with left subtree of depth 2 (root->left->leaf) and right leaf, L=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Left leaf: (0,0). Step 2: Left child node: has one child (leaf) with (0,0). Compute for left child: new_min = 1 + min(0,inf) = 1, new_max = 1 + max(0,inf) = 1 (assuming no right child, treat as inf). Left child result: (1,1). Step 3: Right leaf: (0,0). Step 4: Root: left child (1,1), right child (0,0). Check if children can satisfy L: left max_len=1 ≤ 1, right max_len=0 ≤ 1. Compute new_min = 1 + min(1,0) = 1, new_max = 1 + max(1,0) = 2. Root result: (1,2). Since max_len=2 > L=1, return False.

testCases.input: Tree: root with two leaf children, L=1
Tree: root with left depth 2 and right leaf, L=1
Tree: root with left subtree depth 3 and right subtree depth 2, L=2
Tree: root with left leaf and right subtree depth 2, L=2
Tree: balanced tree of depth 3 (7 nodes), L=3
Tree: skewed left tree of depth 4, L=3
Tree: root with left child (leaf) and right child with two leaves, L=2
Tree: root with both children having depth 2, L=1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Min/Max Path Lengths for a Node
ladders.desc: Recursive function that computes the minimum and maximum path lengths from a node to leaves in its subtree after optimal pruning.
ladders.explanation: This helper function is the core of the DP approach. For a given node and latency constraint L, it returns a tuple (min_len, max_len) representing the shortest and longest possible paths from this node to any leaf in its subtree, assuming we prune subtrees optimally to satisfy L. The intuition is that by computing these bounds bottom-up, we can determine if the subtree rooted at this node can be adjusted to meet L. Algorithm: If node is None, return (inf, -inf) as sentinel values (since no path exists). If node is a leaf (no children), return (0,0) because the path from node to itself has length 0. Otherwise, recursively compute (l_min, l_max) for left child and (r_min, r_max) for right child. Then, check if each child's subtree can be pruned to satisfy L: if l_max > L, then the left subtree cannot satisfy L even after pruning, so we must prune it entirely (treat as inf). Similarly for right. After this check, compute new_min = 1 + min(l_min, r_min) and new_max = 1 + max(l_max, r_max), but use adjusted values for pruned children. Store result in memoization dictionary keyed by node to avoid recomputation. Time complexity per call is O(1) after child computations, but overall O(n) due to recursion. Space is O(n) for recursion depth and memoization. Connection to L0: This function is called recursively from the root to compute the final result. Common mistakes: Forgetting to add 1 for the current node's edge, or not handling sentinel values correctly. Real code pattern: Define a function compute_lengths(node, L, memo) that returns (min_len, max_len).

examples.input: Node with two leaf children, L=2
examples.trace: Step 1: Node is internal. Compute left child (leaf): (0,0). Step 2: Right child (leaf): (0,0). Step 3: Check children: l_max=0 ≤ 2, r_max=0 ≤ 2. Step 4: new_min = 1 + min(0,0) = 1, new_max = 1 + max(0,0) = 1. Return (1,1).

examples.input: Node with left child of depth 2 and right leaf, L=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Compute left child recursively: left child has one leaf child. Leaf: (0,0). For left child: new_min = 1 + min(0,inf) = 1, new_max = 1 + max(0,inf) = 1. Left child result: (1,1). Step 2: Right leaf: (0,0). Step 3: Check children: l_max=1 ≤ 1, r_max=0 ≤ 1. Step 4: new_min = 1 + min(1,0) = 1, new_max = 1 + max(1,0) = 2. Return (1,2).

Node with left (0,1) and right (0,0), L=1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Post-Order Traversal for Bottom-Up Computation
ladders.desc: Using post-order traversal to compute subtree properties from leaves up to the root.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is to traverse the tree in post-order (left, right, root) so that when we process a node, we have already computed results for its children. This enables bottom-up dynamic programming where we build solutions for larger subtrees from smaller ones. Intuition: In tree problems, many properties (like path lengths) depend on children, so processing children first allows us to aggregate information upward. Algorithm: Define a recursive function that first calls itself on the left child, then on the right child, then processes the current node using the children's results. For this problem, we compute min and max path lengths at each node based on children's values. This ensures that we only visit each node once, leading to O(n) time. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack, where h is tree height. Connection to L1: This traversal order is used in the compute_lengths function. Common mistakes: Using pre-order or in-order traversal, which doesn't guarantee children are processed first. Real code pattern: def post_order(node): if not node: return; post_order(node.left); post_order(node.right); process(node).

examples.input: Tree: root with left leaf and right leaf
examples.output: Traversal order: left leaf, right leaf, root
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root. Call post_order on left child (leaf). Leaf has no children, so process leaf (e.g., compute lengths). Step 2: Return to root, call post_order on right child (leaf). Process right leaf. Step 3: Process root using results from left and right.

examples.input: Tree: root with left child (which has a leaf) and right leaf
examples.output: Traversal order: left leaf, left child, right leaf, root
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root. Call post_order on left child. Step 2: Left child calls post_order on its left child (leaf). Process leaf. Step 3: Return to left child, process it. Step 4: Return to root, call post_order on right leaf. Process right leaf. Step 5: Process root.

testCases.input: Root with left and right leaves
Root with left subtree of 2 nodes
Skewed tree of 3 nodes to left
Tree with root, left child with leaf, right child with leaf
testCases.expected: [left leaf, left child, right leaf, right child, root]
[left left, left, right left, right, root]

ladders.title: Building Block — Memoization in Tree Recursion
ladders.desc: Storing results of subtree computations to avoid redundant calculations.
ladders.explanation: Memoization is an optimization technique where we cache the results of expensive function calls and return the cached result when the same inputs occur again. In tree recursion, multiple paths might lead to the same subtree, so memoization prevents recomputation. Intuition: In a tree, each node is unique, but without memoization, we might compute properties for the same node multiple times in different recursive calls (though in this problem, each node is visited once due to post-order, but memoization ensures we don't recompute if called again). Here, we use a dictionary keyed by node reference (or node ID) to store the (min_len, max_len) tuple. When compute_lengths is called on a node, we first check the memo dictionary; if present, return the cached value. Otherwise, compute recursively, store the result, and then return it. This reduces time complexity from exponential to linear in many cases. Time and space: O(n) for storing n entries. Connection to L2: Memoization is applied during the post-order traversal. Common mistakes: Using mutable keys incorrectly, or not accounting for changes in L (here L is constant, so keyed by node alone). Real code pattern: memo = {}; def compute(node): if node in memo: return memo[node]; result = compute_recursively(); memo[node] = result; return result.

examples.input: Tree with two calls to same node
examples.trace: Step 1: First call to compute_lengths for node A. Not in memo, so compute recursively for its children, get result R, store memo[A]=R. Step 2: Second call to compute_lengths for node A. Check memo, find R, return R without recomputation.

examples.input: Tree where a node is ancestor of another
examples.trace: Step 1: Post-order traversal visits leaf nodes first, compute and store in memo. Step 2: Visit parent node, check memo for children (already stored), compute parent result, store in memo. Step 3: Continue up to root.

testCases.input: Node with same L but different tree

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Tree Node Structure and Recursion
ladders.desc: Defining a binary tree node and performing simple recursive traversal.
ladders.explanation: This foundational operation involves defining a tree node structure and writing a basic recursive function to traverse the tree. A binary tree node typically has attributes: value (optional), left child, and right child. In Python, we can define a class TreeNode with __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None). Recursion is a natural way to process trees because each subtree is itself a tree. The base case is when node is None (empty subtree). Recursive case: process the node, then call the function on left and right children. Intuition: Recursion breaks down the problem into smaller subproblems (subtrees) and combines results. For traversal, we can do pre-order (root, left, right), in-order (left, root, right), or post-order (left, right, root). Here, we focus on post-order for bottom-up computation. Time complexity is O(n) for visiting n nodes, space is O(h) for recursion stack. Connection to L3: This operation is used as the skeleton for memoization and DP. Common mistakes: Infinite recursion if base case missing, or not handling None children. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. def traverse(node): if not node: return; traverse(node.left); traverse(node.right); print(node.val).

examples.input: Tree with root value 1, left leaf 2, right leaf 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Call traverse(root). Node is not None. Step 2: Call traverse(left child with value 2). Left child is leaf, so call traverse(None) for its left, then traverse(None) for its right, then print 2. Step 3: Return to root, call traverse(right child with value 3). Similar, print 3. Step 4: Print root value 1.

examples.input: Empty tree
examples.trace: Step 1: Call traverse(None). Base case triggers, return immediately.

testCases.expected: Root with left child 2, right child 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Trees and Path Length
ladders.desc: Understanding binary trees and the concept of path length from root to leaf.
ladders.explanation: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. The top node is the root. A leaf is a node with no children. A path is a sequence of nodes connected by edges, and the path length is the number of edges from one node to another. For example, the path from root to a leaf has length equal to the depth of that leaf (number of edges traversed). Intuition: In tree problems, we often need to compute properties along paths, such as finding the longest or shortest path. This foundational concept is crucial for the pruning problem because we need to ensure all root-to-leaf paths satisfy a latency constraint (max length). To compute path length, start at a node and count edges as you move to a descendant. For a leaf, the path to itself has length 0. For an internal node, the path length to a leaf is 1 plus the path length from its child to that leaf. This recursive definition leads naturally to recursive algorithms. Time and space: Basic understanding, no complexity yet. Connection to L4: This concept is used when defining tree nodes and traversals. Common mistakes: Confusing path length with number of nodes (edges vs nodes), or not considering empty trees. Real code pattern: No code yet, but conceptually: path_length(node) = 0 if node is leaf, else 1 + min(path_length(child)) for some child.

examples.input: Tree: root with left leaf
examples.output: Path from root to left leaf has length 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Root has one edge to left leaf. Step 2: Count edges: 1 edge, so length 1.

examples.input: Tree: root with left child and right leaf, left child has a leaf
examples.output: Path from root to deepest leaf has length 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Root to left child: edge 1. Step 2: Left child to its leaf: edge 2. Total length 2.

testCases.expected: Path length from root to itself: 0

testCases.input: Node with only left subtree of depth 2
Internal node with no leaf descendants
Path from root to internal node
Tree where all leaves at same depth

approaches.name: Approach 3: Greedy Pruning with BFS
approaches.summary: Use a breadth-first search to iteratively prune leaves or nodes that cause path violations, prioritizing nodes deeper in the tree to minimize removals while ensuring all paths meet the constraint. This approach makes local optimal decisions at each step.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Greedy BFS Pruning for Decision Trees with Latency Constraints
ladders.desc: Complete solution using greedy BFS pruning to ensure all root-to-leaf paths satisfy a maximum depth constraint.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires pruning a decision tree so that every root-to-leaf path has depth ≤ L (latency constraint). The greedy BFS approach works by processing nodes level-by-level from bottom-up, removing nodes that cause violations while minimizing tree modifications. Intuition: By starting from the deepest nodes (via BFS reversed order), we make locally optimal decisions—pruning deeper violating nodes first often reduces overall pruning since their removal automatically fixes all descendant paths. The algorithm: 1) Perform BFS to collect nodes in level order. 2) Process nodes from deepest to shallowest. For each node, calculate its depth from root. If it's a leaf and depth > L, prune it (remove from parent). If it's internal and all children are pruned/absent, treat as leaf and prune if depth > L. 3) Repeat until no violations. Time O(n log n) due to sorting nodes by depth; space O(n) for queues and storage. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into a complete system. Common mistakes: Not handling internal nodes becoming leaves after pruning children (need to recalculate), or pruning too aggressively (greedy choice must prioritize deepest nodes). Real pattern: Use deque for BFS, sort nodes by depth descending, maintain parent pointers for removal.

Pseudocode:
function canPruneTree(root, L):
    if not root: return True
    nodes = []
    queue = deque([(root, None, 0)])  # (node, parent, depth)
    while queue:
        node, parent, depth = queue.popleft()
        nodes.append((node, parent, depth))
        for child in node.children:
            queue.append((child, node, depth+1))
    
    # Sort by depth descending
    nodes.sort(key=lambda x: x[2], reverse=True)
    
    pruned = set()
    for node, parent, depth in nodes:
        if node in pruned:
            continue
        # Check if node is effectively a leaf (all children pruned or none)
        effective_leaf = all(child in pruned for child in node.children)
        if effective_leaf and depth > L:
            if parent is None:  # Root violation
                return False
            pruned.add(node)
            # Remove from parent's children
            if parent:
                parent.children.remove(node)
    
    # Final check: all remaining leaves must have depth ≤ L
    for node, parent, depth in nodes:
        if node not in pruned and not node.children and depth > L:
            return False
    return True

examples.input: Tree: root with children A,B; A has child C; B has child D; C and D are leaves. L=2
examples.trace: 1. BFS collects nodes: (root,None,0), (A,root,1), (B,root,1), (C,A,2), (D,B,2). Sort by depth desc: C,D,A,B,root. 2. Process C: depth=2 ≤ L=2, keep. 3. Process D: depth=2 ≤ L, keep. 4. Process A: not effective leaf (C not pruned), keep. 5. Process B: not effective leaf, keep. 6. Root: keep. Final check: leaves C,D depth=2 okay. Return True.

examples.input: Tree: root with child A; A has child B; B has child C (leaf). L=2
examples.trace: 1. BFS: (root,None,0), (A,root,1), (B,A,2), (C,B,3). Sort: C,B,A,root. 2. Process C: depth=3 > L=2, prune C (remove from B). 3. Process B: now effective leaf (C pruned), depth=2 ≤ L, keep. 4. Process A: not effective leaf, keep. 5. Root: keep. Final check: leaf B depth=2 okay. But wait—original leaf C violated, but after pruning, tree valid? Actually, after pruning C, B becomes leaf at depth=2 ≤ L, so tree can be pruned. Output should be True. Let's correct: The algorithm returns True because pruning C fixes violation. Example shows pruning works.

testCases.input: Tree: root with leaf child. L=0
Tree: root with leaf child. L=1
Tree: root with two leaf children. L=1
Tree: root with children A,B; A->C; B->D; C,D leaves. L=2
Tree: root->A->B; A->C; B,C leaves. L=1
Tree: root->A->B; root->C; A,B,C leaves. L=2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bottom-up Node Processing with Depth Check
ladders.desc: Process tree nodes from deepest to shallowest, pruning leaves that exceed depth constraint.

examples.input: Nodes: [(A, depth=3, children=[B]), (B, depth=4, children=[]), (C, depth=2, children=[])]; L=3
examples.trace: Sort by depth: B(4), A(3), C(2). Process B: leaf? yes (no children), depth=4 > L=3 → prune B. Process A: leaf? now yes (B pruned), depth=3 ≤ L → keep. Process C: leaf? yes, depth=2 ≤ L → keep. Final pruned set {B}.

examples.input: Nodes: [(X, depth=1, children=[Y]), (Y, depth=2, children=[Z]), (Z, depth=3, children=[])]; L=2
examples.trace: Sort: Z(3), Y(2), X(1). Process Z: leaf? yes, depth=3 > L=2 → prune Z. Process Y: leaf? now yes (Z pruned), depth=2 ≤ L → keep. Process X: leaf? no (Y not pruned) → keep. Pruned {Z}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Choice: Prune Deepest Violating Nodes First
ladders.desc: The insight to prioritize pruning nodes with greater depth to minimize tree modifications.

examples.input: Violations: [node depth=4, node depth=2]; L=3
examples.trace: Sort violations: [depth=4, depth=2]. Process depth=4: prune it. After pruning, check if depth=2 still violates (it does, 2 ≤ 3? Actually 2 ≤ 3, so not a violation—wait, L=3, depth=2 is okay. So only depth=4 was violating. Pruning it fixes all.

examples.input: Violations: [node depth=5, node depth=6]; L=4
examples.trace: Sort: [6,5]. Prune depth=6 node. Then prune depth=5 node if still violating (5 > 4, yes). So prune both in order.

ladders.title: Building Block — BFS to Collect Nodes with Depths
ladders.desc: Perform breadth-first search on a tree to gather all nodes along with their depths from root.

examples.trace: Node has children A and B. Both are in pruned_set, so all children pruned → effective leaf.
